comments.text: In C++ we don't say "Missing asterisk" we say "error C2664: 'void std::vector<block,std::allocator<_Ty>>::push_back(const block &)': cannot convert argument 1 from 'std::_Vector_iterator<std::_Vector_val<std::_Simple_types<block>>>' to 'block &&'"

listens carefully

videos.title: Falling ladders - why does this happen?
The real science of black holes
Can something go faster than it’s pushed?
Why don't trains make *that* sound anymore?

comments.author: @cicalinarrot
comments.text: Plot twist: he has no idea why that happened and he's genuinely asking, hoping that someone tells him in the comment.

comments.author: @diray3474
comments.text:
- defies physics
-refuses to elaborate
-disappears into the void

comments.author: @heftylad
comments.text: For those wondering, you see how the rods are alternating diagonal? When the downward part of the bottom rod hits the table, it bounces up, pulling the other side of the rod, and therefore that rope, downwards. Rinse and repeat with every rod, all pulling slightly downwards on the ladder, and now it's falling faster than the other.

comments.author: @DoDone-l7c
comments.text:
The angle causes the downward pull of each subsequent bar. When it hits the table the excess energy is used as leverage.
Didn't know this was old.

comments.author: @jeffstarkmann8468
comments.text: "Alright, then. Keep your secrets"

comments.author: @AnoNymous-dh2sv
comments.text: C++ of 2022, is EXTREMELY different to C++ of 20+ years ago. Now it has extremely high level libraries on the DEFAULT spec so you can do things that any extremely high level language like python did by default (like text parsing) and I think if it had those features from the start dozens of popular languages would not even EXIST.
